title: The Reluctant Vegetarian: A Year on Plants
summary: An Australian narrator reflects on a year as a reluctant vegetarian, what changed in her body and her grocery bill, and why the experiment did not end where she expected.
body: A year ago, my doctor told me to cut down on red meat. I had been expecting that conversation for a while, but I'd been putting it off. My cholesterol had been creeping up for three years, and I had been promising to do something about it for almost as long. I left the surgery telling myself I would just eat less steak. By the time I got home, I had decided to try a full year as a vegetarian — partly because half measures had never worked for me before. Twelve months on, I am still a vegetarian, but the reasons are not the reasons I started. The first three months were genuinely hard. I missed bacon. I missed the smell of meat at family lunches. I spent more time in the kitchen than I ever had, because the easy meals I'd relied on for a decade all involved chicken. Several friends asked, with real concern, whether I was getting enough protein. I learned to laugh at this and then to point out that lentils contain more protein per pound than steak. I lost five kilos in those three months, which was nice, but I also felt tired, which was not. The next three months were the turning point. My energy came back, my sleep improved, and a long-standing problem with my joints faded so completely that I stopped noticing it. None of this was the reason I had started. I had started for cholesterol, and indeed my cholesterol had fallen — but the other changes were bigger than I had expected, and they made the diet self-reinforcing. By month six, the question was no longer 'can I stick with this?' but 'why did I wait so long?' The grocery bill was the second surprise. I had assumed vegetarian food would be cheaper, because vegetables are cheaper than meat. In practice, I spent more, because I was buying better-quality produce, more fresh herbs, and a lot of nuts. After about month eight I adjusted, and I now spend slightly less than I used to, but only because I learned how to cook seasonally. People who switch to plant-based diets and expect immediate savings often quit by month four because the bill rises before it falls. The biggest surprise, though, was social. I had not expected eating differently to change my relationships, but it did. Family dinners became a small ongoing negotiation. Some friends found my diet quietly accusing, even though I never preached to anyone. Other friends, ones I had not been close to, suddenly invited me over because they were curious. I have made two new friendships out of this, and I have noticed a small amount of distance enter at least one old one. I cannot in good conscience tell anyone to do what I did. The decision depends on your health, your culture, and how much your social life happens around meat. What I can say is that the experiment was not what I expected it to be, and that the most useful changes came from the parts I had not been looking for. If you are thinking about it, give it six months, not one. The first three months will lie to you about whether it works. The next three will tell you the truth.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect Continuous for Long Ongoing Action
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng thì quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn để nhấn mạnh các quá trình kéo dài trước một mốc quá khứ: 'I had been expecting that conversation', 'My cholesterol had been creeping up for three years', 'I had been promising to do something'. Cấu trúc: had been + V-ing. Khác với quá khứ hoàn thành đơn (had + V3 — kết quả), dạng tiếp diễn nhấn mạnh quá trình và sự kéo dài.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses past perfect continuous to stress long ongoing processes before a past point: 'I had been expecting that conversation', 'My cholesterol had been creeping up for three years', 'I had been promising to do something'. Form: had been + V-ing. Unlike the simple past perfect (had + V3 — result), the continuous form emphasises duration and process.

grammarNotes.title: Third Conditional and Modal Reflection
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng các cấu trúc giả định để phản ánh: 'I had assumed vegetarian food would be cheaper', 'I had not expected eating differently to change my relationships', 'the most useful changes came from the parts I had not been looking for'. Khi suy ngẫm về một quá trình đã qua, các cụm này cho phép so sánh giữa kỳ vọng và thực tế.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses hypothetical constructions to reflect: 'I had assumed vegetarian food would be cheaper', 'I had not expected eating differently to change my relationships', 'the most useful changes came from the parts I had not been looking for'. When reflecting on a past process, these structures contrast expectation with reality.

translationVi: Một năm trước, bác sĩ bảo tôi giảm bớt thịt đỏ. Tôi đã chờ cuộc nói chuyện đó từ lâu, nhưng tôi đã trì hoãn. Cholesterol của tôi đã leo dần lên trong ba năm, và tôi đã hứa sẽ làm gì đó về nó gần như cùng khoảng thời gian. Tôi rời phòng khám tự nhủ chỉ ăn ít bít tết thôi. Khi về đến nhà, tôi đã quyết định thử cả năm ăn chay — một phần vì các biện pháp nửa vời chưa bao giờ hiệu quả với tôi. Mười hai tháng sau, tôi vẫn ăn chay, nhưng lý do không phải là lý do tôi bắt đầu. Ba tháng đầu thực sự khó. Tôi nhớ thịt xông khói. Tôi nhớ mùi thịt ở các bữa trưa gia đình. Tôi dành nhiều thời gian trong bếp hơn bao giờ hết, vì các món dễ làm tôi từng dựa vào một thập kỷ đều có gà. Vài người bạn hỏi, với sự lo lắng thực sự, liệu tôi có đủ protein không. Tôi học cách cười điều này rồi chỉ ra rằng đậu lăng có nhiều protein trên một pound hơn bít tết. Tôi giảm năm cân trong ba tháng đó, dễ chịu, nhưng tôi cũng cảm thấy mệt, điều không dễ chịu. Ba tháng tiếp theo là bước ngoặt. Năng lượng tôi trở lại, giấc ngủ cải thiện, và một vấn đề lâu năm với khớp biến mất hoàn toàn đến mức tôi ngừng để ý đến nó. Không điều nào là lý do tôi đã bắt đầu. Tôi bắt đầu vì cholesterol, và quả thực cholesterol của tôi đã giảm — nhưng các thay đổi khác lớn hơn tôi đã mong đợi, và chúng làm cho chế độ ăn tự củng cố. Đến tháng sáu, câu hỏi không còn là 'tôi có thể giữ được không?' mà là 'sao tôi đã chờ lâu vậy?' Hoá đơn siêu thị là bất ngờ thứ hai. Tôi đã giả định đồ chay sẽ rẻ hơn, vì rau rẻ hơn thịt. Trong thực tế, tôi tiêu nhiều hơn, vì tôi mua rau quả chất lượng cao hơn, nhiều rau thơm tươi hơn, và rất nhiều hạt. Sau khoảng tháng tám, tôi đã điều chỉnh, và bây giờ tôi tiêu ít hơn một chút so với trước, nhưng chỉ vì tôi học cách nấu theo mùa. Những người chuyển sang ăn chay và kỳ vọng tiết kiệm ngay thường bỏ vào tháng bốn vì hoá đơn tăng trước khi giảm. Bất ngờ lớn nhất là về xã hội. Tôi đã không kỳ vọng ăn khác sẽ thay đổi mối quan hệ, nhưng có. Bữa cơm gia đình trở thành cuộc thương lượng nhỏ liên tục. Một số bạn bè thấy chế độ ăn của tôi như ngầm trách móc, dù tôi chưa bao giờ rao giảng với ai. Một số bạn khác, những người tôi chưa thân, đột nhiên mời tôi qua vì họ tò mò. Tôi đã có hai tình bạn mới từ chuyện này, và tôi nhận thấy một khoảng cách nhỏ len vào ít nhất một tình bạn cũ. Tôi không thể, theo lương tâm, bảo ai đó làm điều tôi đã làm. Quyết định phụ thuộc vào sức khoẻ, văn hoá, và đời sống xã hội của bạn xoay quanh thịt bao nhiêu. Điều tôi có thể nói là thí nghiệm không phải là điều tôi đã kỳ vọng, và các thay đổi hữu ích nhất đến từ những phần tôi đã không tìm kiếm. Nếu bạn đang nghĩ về chuyện này, hãy thử sáu tháng, không phải một. Ba tháng đầu sẽ nói dối bạn về việc nó có hiệu quả không. Ba tháng tiếp theo sẽ nói cho bạn sự thật.

questions.prompt: Why did the narrator's doctor want her to cut down on red meat?
options: Her cholesterol had been creeping up for three years
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'My cholesterol had been creeping up for three years.'
questions.hint: A specific medical concern is named.

questions.prompt: What did the narrator decide between leaving the surgery and getting home?
options: To try a full year as a vegetarian
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'By the time I got home, I had decided to try a full year as a vegetarian.'
questions.hint: The decision is more dramatic than the original plan.

questions.prompt: Why does the narrator say half measures had never worked for her?
options: Because she had relied on chicken-based easy meals for a decade
Because she had no medical guidance
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'half measures had never worked for me before.' She implies she needed an all-or-nothing approach.
questions.hint: She does not give a single reason, but her phrasing implies a pattern.

questions.prompt: What did several friends ask with real concern?
options: Whether she was eating any vegetables
Whether she was getting enough protein
Whether she had told her family
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Several friends asked, with real concern, whether I was getting enough protein.'
questions.hint: A specific question that comes up often with new vegetarians.

questions.prompt: What two effects did the first three months have on her body?
options: She gained weight and felt tired
She lost weight and slept perfectly
She lost five kilos but felt tired
She felt energetic and gained weight
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I lost five kilos in those three months, which was nice, but I also felt tired, which was not.'
questions.hint: One positive and one negative effect.

questions.prompt: What happened in the next three months?
options: Her energy came back, her sleep improved, and a long-standing joint problem faded
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'My energy came back, my sleep improved, and a long-standing problem with my joints faded so completely that I stopped noticing it.'

questions.prompt: What was the narrator's original reason for starting?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I had started for cholesterol.'
questions.hint: She names the original medical reason.

options: Vegetarian food was not cheaper at first — the grocery bill went up before it came down
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I had assumed vegetarian food would be cheaper... In practice, I spent more.'

questions.prompt: Why do people often quit by month four?
options: Because the grocery bill rises before it falls and they expect immediate savings
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'People who switch to plant-based diets and expect immediate savings often quit by month four because the bill rises before it falls.'
questions.hint: The reason is financial, not physical.

options: How quickly the joint pain went
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The biggest surprise, though, was social.'

questions.prompt: What two opposite social effects did the narrator experience?
options: She made two new friendships, and noticed distance entering at least one old friendship
Two old friendships strengthened, and two new ones did not happen
Family supported her, but everyone else stopped speaking
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I have made two new friendships out of this, and I have noticed a small amount of distance enter at least one old one.'
questions.hint: Two specific changes — one positive, one negative.

questions.prompt: What advice does the narrator give to someone considering this experiment?
options: Try it for a week first
Give it six months, because the first three months will lie about whether it works
Don't tell anyone you're doing it
Eat fish to make it easier
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'give it six months, not one. The first three months will lie to you about whether it works. The next three will tell you the truth.'

questions.prompt: What overall lesson does the narrator draw from her experiment?
options: She cannot in good conscience tell anyone to copy her — the most useful changes came from parts she hadn't been looking for, and the decision depends on individual context
Only people with high cholesterol should try it
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I cannot in good conscience tell anyone to do what I did' and 'the most useful changes came from the parts I had not been looking for.'
questions.hint: Her conclusion is humble and condition-aware.

cloze.template: A year ago, my doctor told me to cut {{b1}} on red meat. My cholesterol had been creeping up for three years. I left the surgery telling myself I would just eat less steak. By the time I got home, I had decided to try a full year as a vegetarian — partly because {{b2}} measures had never worked for me before. The first three months were genuinely hard. The easy meals I'd relied on for a decade all involved chicken. Several friends asked, with real concern, whether I was getting enough {{b3}}. I lost five kilos, but I also felt {{b4}}. The next three months were the {{b5}} point. My energy came back, my sleep improved, and a long-standing problem with my joints faded. My cholesterol had fallen, but the other changes were bigger than I had expected, and they made the diet self-{{b6}}. The grocery bill was the second surprise. I had assumed vegetarian food would be cheaper. In practice, I spent more. People who expect immediate savings often {{b7}} by month four because the bill rises before it falls. The biggest surprise was social. Some friends found my diet quietly {{b8}}, even though I never preached. I made two new friendships, and I have noticed a small amount of distance enter at least one old one. I cannot in good {{b9}} tell anyone to do what I did. If you are thinking about it, give it six months. The first three months will {{b10}} to you about whether it works. The next three will tell you the truth.

blanks.explanation: 'Cut down on' is the standard fixed phrase for reducing consumption of something.
'Half measures' is the fixed idiom — inadequate, partial efforts.
'Protein' is the specific nutrient most often raised when someone goes vegetarian.
'Tired' is the exact word the narrator uses for her low-energy period.
'Turning point' is the fixed idiom — the moment when a process changes direction.
'Self-reinforcing' is the exact adjective — the diet's benefits made continuing easier.
'Quit' is the exact verb the narrator uses for people who give up.
'Quietly accusing' is the exact unusual phrase the narrator uses — implicit judgement without speech.
'In good conscience' is the fixed phrase meaning with moral integrity.
The narrator personifies the first three months as lying — her exact verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says her most valuable changes came from parts of the experiment she hadn't been looking for. Have you ever made a change for one reason and benefited from something completely unrelated — and does that suggest planning is less important than experimentation?
